title: Decision Trees
description: Learn how decision trees classify observations by recursively splitting data.
takeaways: Decision trees split data using feature-based rules.
Deep trees can overfit training data.

practiceLab.objective: Train a DecisionTreeClassifier on the Iris dataset and observe how max_depth changes training accuracy and tree depth.
instructions: Run the import cell and confirm DecisionTreeClassifier imported! appears.
Run the Iris loading cell and confirm it reports 150 samples and 4 features.
Run the training cell to fit a DecisionTreeClassifier with random_state=42 on X and y.
Confirm the printed Classes: array lists the Iris class labels.
In the final cell, keep max_depth=3, run the cell, and record the printed Accuracy and Tree depth.
Change max_depth to 1, re-run the cell, and compare accuracy and tree depth with the previous result.
Change max_depth to None, re-run the cell, and note how accuracy and tree depth change again.
Decide which depth setting produced the deepest tree and which produced the lowest accuracy.
hints: Create the model with DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=..., random_state=42).
Train with model.fit(X, y) and score with model.score(X, y).
Use model.get_depth() to read the grown tree depth after fitting.
This notebook scores on the same data used for training (X, y), not a held-out test set.
practiceLab.expectedOutput: Printed accuracy values and tree depths for max_depth settings such as 1, 3, and None on the Iris dataset.
